elements.sent-2411-001: This thesis deals with the problem of communicating and storing non-sequential data.
elements.sent-2411-002: We investigate this problem through the lens of lossless source coding, also sometimes referred to as lossless compression, from both an algorithmic and information-theoretic perspective.
elements.sent-2411-003: Lossless compression algorithms typically preserve the ordering in which data points are compressed.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, there are data types where order is not meaningful, such as collections of files, rows in a database, nodes in a graph, and, notably, datasets in machine learning applications.
elements.sent-2411-005: Compressing with traditional algorithms is possible if we pick an order for the elements and communicate the corresponding ordered sequence.
elements.sent-2411-006: However, unless the order information is somehow removed during the encoding process, this procedure will be sub-optimal, because the order contains information and therefore more bits are used to represent the source than are truly necessary.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this work we give a formal definition for non-sequential objects as random sets of equivalent sequences, which we refer to as Combinatorial Random Variables (CRVs).
elements.sent-2411-008: The definition of equivalence, formalized as an equivalence relation, establishes the non-sequential data type represented by the CRV.
elements.sent-2411-009: The achievable rates of CRVs is fully characterized as a function of the equivalence relation as well as the data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-010: The optimal rates of CRVs are achieved within the family of Random Permutation Codes (RPCs) developed in later chapters.
elements.sent-2411-011: RPCs randomly select one-of-many possible sequences that can represent the instance of the CRV.
elements.sent-2411-012: The selection is done through sampling with bits-back coding \cite{townsend2019practical, frey1996free and asymmetric numeral systems \cite{duda2009asymmetric , and guarantees the achievability of the optimal rate.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Specialized RPCs are given for the case of multisets, graphs, and partitions/clusterings, providing new algorithms for compression of databases, social networks, and web data in the JSON file format.
elements.sent-2411-014: The computational and memory complexity of RPCs is discussed and shown to be attractive for the applications considered.

elements.sent-2411-047: To illustrate, consider the problem of representing a set of $n$ distinct elements, from a total of $m$ possible candidates, on a modern digital computer.
elements.sent-2411-048: A common solution is to represent the set as a sequence of its elements, such as in an array or list, in decreasing or increasing order (i.e., sorted).
elements.sent-2411-049: However, a set is a mathematical object void of any ordering between elements: any of the $n!$ possible orderings of the sequence would be valid representatives.
elements.sent-2411-050: One can therefore hypothesize a communication protocol where only the first $n-1$ elements are stored, while each of the $m - (n-1)$ possible values for the last element are mapped to one of the $(n-1)!$ orderings of the first $n-1$ elements.
elements.sent-2411-051: The value of the last element need not be stored, and can be deduced directly from the ordering of the first $n-1$ elements, assuming the map is known.
elements.sent-2411-052: This example illustrate the key observation underlying this thesis: a non-sequential object can be stored using less bits than what is required to represent an arbitrary sequence of its elements; by encoding information in the order between elements in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-053: Throughout this manuscript we will identify different types of non-sequential objects including a variety of graphs, generalizations of sets (i.e., multisets), partitions, clusters, and certain families of permutations of integers.
elements.sent-2411-054: For each we will establish the minimal number of bits required to store such objects through the lens of information theory \cite{shannon1948mathematical,cover1999elements .}
elements.sent-2411-055: We will show all these objects can be unified under a common framework (\Cref{chapter:crv-rpc ) allowing us to develop computationally efficient compression algorithms for these data types.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Encoding information in the order between elements of a sequence is second-nature to human beings.
elements.sent-2411-057: In scholarly writings, the expected contribution of an individual is communicated to the academic community via their position in the author-list.
elements.sent-2411-058: When viewing the results of a competition, we expect the ordering to encode the ranking of players.
elements.sent-2411-059: Changing the order between words in a sentence can drastically alter the overall meaning (at least in some languages).
elements.sent-2411-060: There are situations where we would like to avoid having to explicitly choose a specific ordering.
elements.sent-2411-061: For example, an author-list where all persons contributed equally, or the listing of players in a team.
elements.sent-2411-062: Similarly, politicians of bilingual countries, e.g., Canada, often go through great lengths during public speeches to avoid giving preferential treatment to a single language, by switching back and forth between both languages.
elements.sent-2411-063: To remove information from the ordering it is common to decide the order through a random coin toss.
elements.sent-2411-064: The hope of the transmitter is that the receiver, knowing the order was decided randomly, will be less likely to extract meaning from the order between elements.
elements.sent-2411-065: The decision of the sequence order is outsourced to a source of randomness not controlled by the transmitter.
elements.sent-2411-066: The solution proposed in the previous paragraph, as well as the family of algorithms developed in this thesis, Random Permutation Codes, makes use of the following fact defining the initial seed of our work: from an information-theoretic point of view, a randomly ordered sequence carries the same information as a non-sequential collection of the same elements.. \changed{.}

elements.sent-2411-105: This chapter reviews the central topic of this thesis: the design of algorithms for representing data, with perfect fidelity, in digital media.
elements.sent-2411-106: This problem is formulated following the developments of Shannon \cite{shannon1948mathematical into what is now known as Information Theory, and, more specifically in our case, Source Coding.}
elements.sent-2411-107: In this classic setting, data and information are viewed through a statistical and probabilistic lens, formalized as a random variable with a known discrete probability law.
elements.sent-2411-108: The amount of information carried by this mathematical object is completely defined by the probability law, being completely agnostic to the semantics of the data itself.
elements.sent-2411-109: Under this theory, the resources required to store and transmit text, numerical quantities, high-resolution images and videos, or any other arbitrary data, are equal, as long as the probability law governing their appearance in our observations is the same.
elements.sent-2411-110: A common misconception amongst practitioners is that the average number of bits required to store a single observation is lower bounded by a quantity known as the entropy.
elements.sent-2411-111: This is true only on average and for an infinite number of samples.
elements.sent-2411-112: It is possible to communicate a sample from a data source using less bits than the entropy, on average, when the number of samples is finite, as shown in \Cref{subsec:rates-below-entropy-are-achievable .}
elements.sent-2411-113: Let $X^n \sim P_{X^n}$ be a sequence of discrete random variables, with alphabet $\X^n$, representing a random sample of the data source.
elements.sent-2411-114: The objective of lossless source coding is to find a binary representation for the elements of $\X^n$, under a set of given constraints, such that any $x^n \in \X^n$ can be recovered from its representation unambiguously and without loss of information.
elements.sent-2411-115: This is achieved through the use of a lossless source code.
elements.sent-2411-116: The codeword of $x^n \in \X^n$ is the image $C(x^n) \in \binstrs$.
elements.sent-2411-117: The codebook is the set of codewords $C(\X^n) \subset \binstrs$.
elements.sent-2411-118: The number of bits in the binary string $C(x^n)$ is called the code length of $x^n$ and is abbreviated as $\ell_{x^n}$ when the code is clear from context.
elements.sent-2411-119: Lossless compression is concerned with finding codes that assign codewords of small lengths for the purpose of storing and transmitting data.
elements.sent-2411-120: The restriction on length can take on many forms with the most common being minimizing the average- or worst-case length.
elements.sent-2411-121: In this thesis we will restrict our discussion to the average case, defined by the following quantity which we refer to as the rate.
elements.sent-2411-122: A lossless code assigns a binary string to each element of $\X^n$.
elements.sent-2411-123: For any code $C$, if there exists an element $s \in \binstrs$ which is not present in the codebook, $s \notin C(\X^n)$, and has length smaller than some code-word, $\abs{s < \ell_{x^n}$, then redefining $C(x^n) \defeq s$ will decrease the rate.}
elements.sent-2411-124: The recursive application of this argument implies that a code achieving the smallest rate must assign code-words sequentially based on length, with ties broken arbitrarily.
elements.sent-2411-125: Codes achieving the smallest rate for a given data source are referred to as optimal codes.
elements.sent-2411-126: Sort the elements of $\X^n$ by their probabilities under $P_{X^n}$, in descending order, breaking ties arbitrarily.
elements.sent-2411-127: Assign code words sequentially, ordered by their lengths, starting from the most probable sequence, $\argmax_{x^n \in \X^n P_{X^n (x^n)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: An optimal sequential code can be implemented via a pair of encoding and decoding functions that specify the next codeword as a function of the current codeword, the incoming symbol, and the number of symbols seen so far $i \in [n]$.
elements.sent-2411-129: The signatures of the pair of functions are \begin{equation}\label{eq:codes-encode-decode} \encode \colon \binstrs \times \X \times [n] \mapsto \binstrs, \decode \colon \binstrs \times [n] \mapsto \X \times \binstrs.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-130: As it stands, encoding and decoding must be done via table or dictionary lookups.

elements.sent-2411-176: The best performing code in this family is the solution to the following optimization problem, \begin{equation}\label{opt:prefix-free-code}  \min_C \quad R(C, P_{X^n}) = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \E{\ell_{X^n}} \suchthat \quad \ell_x \in \Naturals, \sum_{x \in \X} 2^{-\ell_x} \leq 1 \end{equation} The code-word length of the extended code equals the sum of symbol code-word lengths due to concatenation, \begin{equation}\ell_{x^n} = \sum_{i=1}^n \ell_{x_i}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-177: The objective function can be rewritten to expose the mixture of marginals, \begin{equation}\frac{1}{n} \cdot \E{\ell_{X^n}} = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^n \E{\ell_{X_i}} = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{x \in \X}P_{X_i}(x)\cdot\ell_x =\sum_{x \in \X}\ell_x \cdot \left(\frac{1}{n}\cdot\sum_{i=1}^n P_{X_i}(x)\right).\end{equation}.

elements.chapter-2411-004: Entropy Coding with Asymmetric Numeral Systems
elements.sent-2411-231: In \Cref{chapter:lossless-source-coding we showed that the rates achievable by prefix-free and extended codes approach the entropy of the source for i.i.d.\ random variables.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Asymmetric Numeral Systems (ANS) \cite{duda2009asymmetric is a family of codes capable of achieving rates very close to the entropy for large sequence lengths.}
elements.sent-2411-233: At a high level, ANS encoding works by mapping sequences of symbols to a single, large, natural number, called the state, in a reversible way so that the data and state before encoding can be easily recovered.
elements.sent-2411-234: ANS requires access to a quantized probability distribution (\Cref{def:quantized-pmf ) for both encoding and decoding, which we will often refer to a the model.}
elements.sent-2411-235: Symbols are decoded in the opposite order in which they were encoded, making ANS act as a stack, a last-in-first-out data structure, in contrast to the coding schemes discussed in \Cref{chapter:lossless-source-coding which are queue-like or first-in-first-out.}
elements.sent-2411-236: The construction of ANS guarantees that any non-negative integer is a valid state.
elements.sent-2411-237: This allows ANS to be used as a random variate generator.
elements.sent-2411-238: The state is randomly initialized to a large non-negative integer and successive decode operations are applied, generating a sequence from the chosen probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-239: The state need not have been randomly initialized, but may instead have been constructed from a sequence of encode steps from a previous encoding task, as the origin of the state is irrelevant as long as it is integer.
elements.sent-2411-240: This allows the sampling distribution to differ from the distribution used for the previous task.
elements.sent-2411-241: In this use case, the ANS state is thus used as a random seed, and because decoding/sampling removes approximately bits from the state, the random seed is slowly consumed as symbols are sampled.
elements.sent-2411-242: The operation is invertible in the sense that the random seed can be recovered by encoding the generated symbols back into the ANS state.
elements.sent-2411-243: In real-world applications the true data distribution is rarely available.
elements.sent-2411-244: Instead, an estimate is computed from empirical observations.
elements.sent-2411-245: We delay this discussion until later in the section and assume, initially, that $P_{X^n}$ is known.
elements.sent-2411-246: ANS forms a central component of the compression algorithms developed in \Cref{chapter:roc , \Cref{chapter:rcc , and \Cref{chapter:rec of this thesis.}}}
elements.sent-2411-247: In what follows, we discuss an idealized, mathematical, version of ANS.

elements.sent-2411-486: Lossless compression algorithms typically preserve the ordering of compressed symbols in the input sequence.
elements.sent-2411-487: Formally, these may be expressed as a mathematical object known as a multiset: a generalization of a set that allows for repetition of elements.
elements.sent-2411-488: Compressing a multiset with an entropy coder is possible if we somehow order its elements and communicate the corresponding ordered sequence.
elements.sent-2411-489: The fundamental limits of multiset compression are well understood and were first investigated by \cite{varshney2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-490: The information content of a non-trivial multiset is strictly less than that of a sequence with the same elements, by the number of bits required to represent an ordering, or permutation, of the elements.
elements.sent-2411-491: However, previous optimal-rate algorithms for multiset compression have computation time which scales linearly with the size of the alphabet from which elements are drawn \cite{Steinruecken2016-oy .}
elements.sent-2411-492: In this chapter, we show how to compress multisets of statistically exchangeable symbols at an optimal rate, with computational complexity independent of the alphabet size, by converting an existing algorithm for sequences into one for multisets.
elements.sent-2411-493: This enables us to compress fixed-size multisets of independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) symbols\footnote{Any i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-494: The key insight is to avoid encoding the multiset directly, and instead encode a random sequence containing the same elements as the multiset, in $\Omega(n)$ steps, where $n$ is the sequence and multiset size.
elements.sent-2411-495: This can be done by extending the bits-back coding algorithms \cite{frey1996free, frey1997,townsend2019practical discussed in \Cref{chapter:bits-back , where encoding and decoding operations are interleaved during compression and decompression.}}
elements.sent-2411-496: During compression, symbols are sampled without replacement from the multiset and encoded sequentially in the order they are sampled.
elements.sent-2411-497: Sampling is done using an ANS decoder, with the already compressed information used as the random seed for the sample via the ANS state.
elements.sent-2411-498: This procedure is invertible, because the bits used as the random seed can be losslessly recovered during decompression, using an ANS encoder.
elements.sent-2411-499: The sampling step consumes bits, reducing the message length by exactly the number of bits needed to represent a permutation.
elements.sent-2411-500: Our method is attractive in settings where the alphabet size is large, and the symbol distribution assigns most probability mass to a sparse subset of the alphabet.
elements.sent-2411-501: We anticipate that databases and other unordered structured data, in formats like JavaScript Object Notation (JSON), might be realistic use-cases, while for multisets of large objects, such as images or video files, the savings are marginal.
elements.sent-2411-502: We investigate these settings experimentally in \Cref{sec:roc-experiments .}
elements.sent-2411-503: Although our method works for any source of exchangeable symbols, the following sections assumes symbols are i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-504: To the best of our knowledge, there are no previous works that present a method which is both computationally feasible and rate-optimal for compressing multisets of exchangeable symbols with large alphabets.
elements.sent-2411-505: The fundamental limits on lossless compression of multisets were investigated in \cite{varshney2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-506: For finite alphabets, a sequence can be decomposed into a multiset $\M$ (also known as a type) and a permutation conditioned on the multiset.
elements.sent-2411-507: With this, the authors are able to prove a combinatorial bound on the entropy: $H(\M) \leq \Omega(\abs{\X \cdot \log\abs{\M )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-508: However, a practical algorithm for achieving this bound is not presented.
elements.sent-2411-509: A rate-optimal algorithm for compressing multisets of i.i.d\ symbols was put forth in \cite{Steinruecken2016-oy .}
elements.sent-2411-510: There, the information content of the multiset is decomposed recursively over the entire alphabet, allowing it to be interfaced to an entropy coder known as Arithmetic Coding \cite{witten1987arithmetic .}
elements.sent-2411-511: Alternatively, this can also be achieved through Poissonification, even for arbitrary-sized multisets \cite{steinruecken2014b, yang2014compression, yang2017minimax .}
elements.sent-2411-512: Although rate-optimal, the computational complexity of these methods scales linearly with alphabet size.
elements.sent-2411-513: Another approach \cite{gripon2012compressing, Steinruecken2014-zs, reznik2011coding is to convert multisets of sequences (e.g., cryptographic hashes) to an order-invariant data structure, such as a tree, that is losslessly compressible.}
elements.sent-2411-514: These methods can compress arbitrary multisets of i.i.d.\ symbols by first encoding each symbol with a prefix-free code and constructing the order-invariant data-structure from the multiset of resulting code-words.
elements.sent-2411-515: If encoding each symbol individually is optimal (e.g., Huffman coding for dyadic sources), then the method presented in \cite{Steinruecken2014-zs is optimal and has the same complexity as our method.}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \caption{$\bstinsert$ and $\bstremove$ of symbol $x$ with BST $\B$.} \begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth} \DontPrintSemicolon \SetKwFunction{InsertFunc}{$\bstinsert$} \SetKwProg{Fn}{}{:}{} \Fn{\InsertFunc{$\B$, $x$}}{ $(n, y, \B_L, \B_R) = \B$\; \uIf{$x < y$}{ $\B_L = \bstinsert(\B_L, x)$\; } \uElseIf{$x > y$}{ $\B_R = \bstinsert(\B_R, x)$\; } \KwRet $(n+1, y, \B_L, \B_R)$\; } \end{minipage} \label{alg:insert-remove} \begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth} \DontPrintSemicolon \SetKwFunction{RemoveFunc}{$\bstremove$} \SetKwProg{Fn}{}{:}{} \Fn{\RemoveFunc{$\B$, $x$}}{ $(n, y, \B_L, \B_R) = \B$\; \uIf{$x < y$}{ $\B_L = \bstremove(\B_L, x)$\; } \uElseIf{$x > y$}{ $\B_R = \bstremove(\B_R, x)$\; } \KwRet $(n-1, y, \B_L, \B_R)$\; } \end{minipage}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: [H] \caption{$\flookup$ of symbol $x$, and $\rlookup$ of index $i$, in $\B$.} \begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth} \DontPrintSemicolon \SetKwFunction{FLookupFunc}{$\flookup$} \SetKwProg{Fn}{}{:}{} \Fn{\FLookupFunc{$\B$, $x$}}{ $(n, y, \B_L, \B_R) = \B$\; $(n_R, n_L) = (\abs{\B_R}, \abs{\B_L})$\; $(c_y, p_y) = (n_L, n - (n_L + n_R))$\; \uIf{$x = y$}{ $(c_x, p_x) = (c_y, p_y)$\; } \uElseIf{$x < y$}{ $(c_x, p_x) = \flookup(\B_L, x)$\; } \uElseIf{$x > y$}{ $(c, p_x) = \flookup(\B_R, x)$\; $c_x = c - (n - n_R)$\; } \KwRet $(c_x, p_x)$ } \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth} \DontPrintSemicolon \SetKwFunction{RLookupFunc}{$\rlookup$} \SetKwProg{Fn}{}{:}{} \Fn{\RLookupFunc{$\B$, $i$}}{ $(n, y, \B_L, \B_R) = \B$\; $(n_R, n_L) = (\abs{\B_R}, \abs{\B_L})$\; $(c_y, p_y) = (n_L, n - (n_L + n_R))$\; \uIf{$c_y \leq i < c_y + p_y$}{ $(x, c_x, p_x) = (y, c_y, p_y) $\; } \uElseIf{$i < c_y $}{ $(c_x, p_x) = \rlookup(\B_L, i)$ } \uElseIf{$i \geq c_y + p_y$}{ $i' = i - (n - n_R)$\; $(c, p_x) = \rlookup(\B_R, i')$\; $c_x = c + (n - n_R)$\; } \KwRet $(x, c_x, p_x)$ } \end{minipage}

elements.section-2411-018: ROC and the Method of Types
elements.sent-2411-645: A type $\hat{P [x^n]$ of a sequence of length $n$, in the method of types \cite{cover1999elements , is the empirical distribution with probabilities $\hat{P [x^n](x) \defeq \frac{1 {n \sum_{i=1 ^n \1\{x_i = x\}$, constructed from counting the occurrence of symbols in the sequence.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-646: The type class $T(\hat{P [x^n])$ of a type $\hat{P [x^n]$ is the set of all sequences of that type.}}
elements.sent-2411-647: The number of sequences in a type class is equal to the multinomial coefficient associated to that type, \begin{equation}\abs{T(\hat{P}[x^n])} = \frac{n!}{\prod_{x \in \X}(n\cdot\hat{P}[x^n](x))!}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-648: Multisets have a one-to-one mapping with types.
elements.sent-2411-650: The type class is exactly the set of all permutations of $x^n$, the equivalence class of sequences.
elements.sent-2411-651: As far as assigning probabilities to these objects, we can reason about them as equivalent to one another.
elements.sent-2411-652: It is known that, as $n \rightarrow \infty$, the probability of the multiset converges to a delta function \cite{cover1999elements over a single multiset.}
elements.sent-2411-653: This is intuitive from a statistical standpoint: if the sequence length is large, and the symbols are i.i.d., then the empirical distribution, must approach the true data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-654: To see how, note that the negative log-probability of a sequence under an arbitrary i.i.d.\ distribution, $Q_{X^n = \prod_i Q_X$, can be written as a function of its type, \begin{equation}\label{eq:method-of-types-prob} -\frac{1}{n}\cdot\log Q_{X^n}(x^n) = -\frac{1}{n}\cdot\log \prod_{i=1}^n Q_X(x_i) = -\frac{1}{n}\cdot\log \prod_{x \in \X} Q_X(x)^{n \cdot \hat{P}[x^n](x)} = - \sum_{x \in \X} \hat{P}[x^n](x) \cdot \log Q_X(x) = H(\hat{P}[x^n]) + \KL{\hat{P}[x^n]}{Q_X}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-655: Second, the size of the equivalence class can be upper bounded by considering the probability of the equivalence class under the empirical distribution, \begin{equation}\sum_{x^n \in [x^n]} \hat{P}[x^n](x^n) = \abs{T(\hat{P}[x^n])} \cdot \hat{P}[x^n](z^n) = \abs{T(\hat{P}[x^n])} \cdot 2^{-n \cdot H(\hat{P}[x^n])},\end{equation} for any $z^n \in [x^n]$, where the last step follows from \Cref{eq:method-of-types-prob with $Q_X =\hat{P [x^n]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-656: Since $\hat{P [x^n](x^n)$ is a probability mass function, then the sum above must be upper-bounded by $1$, implying \begin{equation}\abs{T(\hat{P}[x^n])} \leq 2^{n \cdot H(\hat{P}[x^n])}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-657: This allows us to write, for any $z^n \in [x^n]$, \begin{equation}P_\M(\M) = \sum_{x^n \in [x^n]} P_{X^n}(x^n) = \abs{T(\hat{P}[x^n])} \cdot P_{X^n}(z^n) \leq 2^{-n \cdot \KL{\hat{P}[x^n]}{P_X}},\end{equation} which is non-zero for $n \rightarrow \infty$ only when $\hat{P [x^n] = P_X$.}

elements.sent-2411-665: In this section we present experiments on synthetically generated multisets with known source distribution, multisets of grayscale images with lossy codecs, and collections of JSON maps represented as a multiset of multisets.
elements.sent-2411-666: We used the ANS implementation in the Craystack library \cite{townsend2020a for all experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-667: Here, synthetically generated multisets are compressed to provide evidence of the computational complexity, and optimal compression rate of the method.
elements.sent-2411-668: We grow the alphabet size $|\X|$ while sampling from the source in a way that guarantees a fixed number of unique symbols $m = 512$.
elements.sent-2411-669: The alphabet $\X$ is always a subset of $\mathbb{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-670: For each run, we generate a multiset with $m=512$ unique symbols, and use a skewed distribution, sampled from a Dirichlet prior with coefficients $\alpha_k = k$ for $k=1,\ldots,|\X|$, as the distribution $P_X$.

elements.subsection-2411-015: Collection of JSON maps as nested multisets
elements.sent-2411-690: The method can be nested to compress a multiset of multisets, by performing an additional sampling step that first chooses which inner multiset to compress.
elements.sent-2411-691: In this section we show results for a collection of JSON maps $\M \defeq \{\J_1, \dots, \J_{|\M|}\}$, where each map $\J_i \defeq \{(k_1, v_1), \dots, (k_{|\J_i| , v_{|\J_i| )\}$ is itself a multiset of key-value pairs.}}
elements.sent-2411-692: To compress, a depth-first approach is taken.
elements.sent-2411-693: First, some $\J \in \M$ is sampled without replacement using an ANS decode.
elements.sent-2411-694: Key-value pairs are then sampled from $\J$, also without replacement, using ANS decode, and compressed to the ANS state until $\J$ is depleted.
elements.sent-2411-695: This procedure repeats, until the outer multiset $\M$ is empty.
elements.sent-2411-696: Assuming all maps are unique, the maximum number of savable bits is \begin{equation}\label{eq:nested-savings} \log |\M|! + \sum_{i=1}^{|\M|} \log |\J_i|!.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-697: The collection of JSON maps is composed of public GitHub user data taken from a release of the Zstandard project\footnote{\url{https://github.com/facebook/zstd/releases/tag/v1.1.3 .}}
elements.sent-2411-698: All key-value elements were cast to strings, for simplicity, and are encoded as UTF-8 bytes using a uniform distribtuion.
elements.sent-2411-699: The theoretical limit curve shows the maximum savable bits with nesting, i.e.\ \Cref{eq:nested-savings .}
elements.sent-2411-700: Note that, without nesting, the theoretical limit would be that of MNIST (i.e. $\log |\M|!$).
elements.sent-2411-701: The method gets very close to the maximum possible savings, for various numbers of JSON maps.
elements.sent-2411-702: The rate savings were small, but these could be improved by using a better technique to encode the UTF-8 strings.
elements.sent-2411-703: Assuming $\J$ represents the JSON map in $\M$ with the largest number of key-value pairs, and that the time complexity of comparing two JSON maps is \(\Omega(|\J|)\), the complexity of the four BST operations for the outer multiset is \(\Omega(|\M| \cdot |\J| \cdot \log|\M|)\).
elements.sent-2411-704: The overall expected time complexity for both encoding and decoding is therefore \begin{equation}\Omega(|\M| \cdot |\J| \cdot (\log|\M| + \log|\J|))\end{equation} It is possible to reduce this by performing the inner sampling steps in parallel, or by speeding up the JSON map comparisons.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Lossless Neural Compression on Binarized MNIST
elements.sent-2411-705: Originally, BB-ANS was introduced as an entropy coder for latent variable models (LVM), such as Gaussian mixtures, that are learned from source samples (see \cite{townsend2020a and \Cref{chapter:bits-back-lvms for a detailed discussion).}}

elements.sent-2411-707: The average bit-length achieved by BB-ANS is an upper-bound on the cross-entropy between the LVM and the true source distribution, and is equal to the Negative Evidence Lower Bound \cite{townsend2020a .}
elements.sent-2411-708: We used the pre-trained model and code made publicly available by the author\footnote{\url{https://github.com/bits-back/bits-back .}}
elements.sent-2411-709: First, an ANS decode is performed to select a binarized MNIST image for compression.
elements.sent-2411-710: Then, BB-ANS is applied as described in the experimental section of \cite{townsend2020a .}
elements.sent-2411-711: This process is repeated, until all $10,000$ images are compressed.
elements.sent-2411-712: We compared the average bit-length with and without the invertible sampling step.
elements.sent-2411-713: Since the images are all unique, the maximum theoretical savings is $\log(10,000!) \text{ bits \approx 14\ \text{kB}$.}
elements.sent-2411-714: This represents a potential savings of $7.6\%$, which is achieved by our method, at the cost of only $10\%$ extra computation time on average.
elements.sent-2411-715: As discussed in \cite{varshney2006 , a sequence can be viewed as a multiset (frequency count of symbols) paired with a permutation (the order of symbols in the multiset).}
elements.sent-2411-716: In this sense, our method implicitly pairs a multiset with a permutation that is incrementally built through sampling without replacement.
elements.sent-2411-717: Through this pairing, we convert a computationally intractable problem (compressing a multiset) into a tractable one (compressing a sequence), while still achieving the optimal rate.
elements.sent-2411-718: This general technique of making a compression problem easier, by augmenting the data in some way, is known as bits-back coding (see Related Work section).
elements.sent-2411-719: Other fruitful applications of bits-back coding may exist which have yet to be discovered.
elements.sent-2411-720: The stack-like nature of ANS precludes random access to symbols in the compressed multiset, as well as streaming multiset communication, because the full multiset must be known before encoding can begin.
elements.sent-2411-721: Methods may exist which allow streaming and/or random access, and this may be another interesting research direction.
elements.sent-2411-722: We see a number of potential generalizations of the method we have presented.
elements.sent-2411-723: Firstly, as suggested in the introduction, the independence assumption can be relaxed: the method can also be used with an adaptive sequence compressor, as long as the implied model over symbols is exchangeable (that is, the probability mass function is invariant to permutations of its inputs).
elements.sent-2411-724: This condition is equivalent to the adaptive decoder not depending on the order of previously observed symbols, i.e.,\ at each decoding step the symbol codec only has access to the multiset of symbols observed so far.
elements.sent-2411-725: Two examples of distributions over multisets in which symbols are exchangeable but not i.i.d.\ are given in \cite{Steinruecken2016-oy .}
elements.sent-2411-726: The first is multisets of symbols drawn i.i.d.\ from an unknown distribution, where the distribution itself is drawn from a Dirichlet prior.
elements.sent-2411-727: The unknown distribution is effectively learned during decoding.
elements.sent-2411-728: The second is uniformly distributed K-combinations (or submultisets) of some fixed ambient multiset.
elements.sent-2411-729: One way to generate a uniformly distributed K-combination is to sample elements without replacement from the ambient multiset.
elements.sent-2411-730: It is possible to efficiently compress K-combinations using an extension of the method in this paper, taking advantage of the fact that the ambient multiset is static and avoiding materializing it explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-731: We leave more detailed discussion of this to future work.
elements.sent-2411-732: As well as using the method with adaptive codecs, it would also be interesting to explore applications to more elaborate multiset-like structures.
elements.sent-2411-733: For example, multigraphs, hypergraphs, and more sophisticated tree structured files, such as the JSON example in the experiments section, which may be represented as nested multisets all the way down.
elements.sent-2411-734: A clustering is a collection of pairwise disjoint sets, called clusters, used throughout science and engineering to group data under context-specific criteria.
elements.sent-2411-735: A clustering can be decomposed conceptually into two parts of differing nature.
elements.sent-2411-736: The data set, created by the set union of all clusters, and the assignments, indicating which elements belong to which cluster.
elements.sent-2411-737: This chapter is concerned with the lossless communication and storage of the assignment information, for arbitrary data sets, from an information theoretic and algorithmic viewpoint.
elements.sent-2411-738: Communicating clusters appears as a fundamental problem in modern vector similarity databases such as FAISS \cite{johnson2019billion .}
elements.sent-2411-739: FAISS is a database designed to store vectors of large dimensionality, usually representing pre-trained embeddings, for similarity search.
elements.sent-2411-740: Given a query vector, FAISS returns a set of the $k$-nearest neighbors \cite{lloyd1982least available in the database under some pre-defined distance metric (usually the L2 distance).}
elements.sent-2411-741: Returning the exact set requires an exhaustive search over the entire database for each query vector which quickly becomes intractable in practice.
elements.sent-2411-742: FAISS can instead return an approximate solution by performing a two-stage search on a coarse and fine grained set of database vectors.
elements.sent-2411-743: The database undergoes a training phase where vectors are clustered into sets and assigned a representative (i.e., a centroid).
elements.sent-2411-744: FAISS first selects the $k^\prime$-nearest clusters, $k^\prime < k$, based on the distance of the query to the centroids, and then performs an exhaustive search within them to return the approximate $k$-nearest neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-745: The cluster assignments must be stored to enable cluster-based approximate searching.
elements.sent-2411-746: In contrast to a class, a cluster is distinguishable only by the elements it contains, and is void of any labelling.
elements.sent-2411-747: However, cluster assignments are often stored alongside the data set in the form of artificially generated labels.
elements.sent-2411-748: Current numbers \cite{chen2010approximate, martinez2016revisiting, babenko2014additive, jegou2010product, huijben2024residual suggest that labelling, for the sake of clustering, can represent the majority of bits spent for communication and storage in a typical use case, and will become the dominating factor as the performance of lossy compression algorithms, used to store the high-dimensional vectors, improves.}
elements.sent-2411-749: In this chapter we show how to communicate and store cluster assignments without creating artificial labels, providing substantial storage savings for vector similarity search applications.
elements.sent-2411-750: Assignments are implicitly represented by a cycle of a permutation defined by the order between encoded elements.
elements.sent-2411-751: Our method, Random Cycle Coding (RCC), uses bits-back coding \cite{townsend2019practical to pick the order in which data points are encoded.}
elements.sent-2411-752: The choice of orderings is restricted to the set of permutations having disjoint cycles with elements equal to some cluster.
elements.sent-2411-753: RCC is optimal for distributions over assignments that assign probability proportional to the product of cluster sizes.
elements.sent-2411-754: In the sense given by \cite{severo2023compressing , it achieves the Shannon bound \cite{cover1999elements in bit savings for the class of bits-back algorithms.}}
elements.sent-2411-755: The worst-case computational complexity of RCC is quasi-linear in the largest cluster size and requires no training or machine learning techniques.
elements.sent-2411-756: Let $X^n = (X_1, \dots, X_n)$ be a sequence of independent random variables $X_i$ with common, but arbitrary, alphabet $\X$.
elements.sent-2411-757: Throughout we assume that a total ordering can be defined for $\X$, i.e., elements of the set can be compared and ranked/sorted according to some predefined criteria (e.g., lexicographical ordering).
elements.sent-2411-758: We assume no repeats happen in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-759: This is motivated by applications where elements are high-dimensional vectors such as embeddings or images where repeats are unlikely to happen.
elements.sent-2411-760: We are interested in the setting where the elements of $X^n$ are grouped into a random clustering $\Pi$ (\Cref{def:clustering ).}
elements.sent-2411-761: The order between elements in a cluster is irrelevant and clusters are void of labels.
elements.sent-2411-762: Conditioned on the sequence $X^n=x^n$ the clustering $\Pi$ defines a partition of the data set $\SetDataSet = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-763: The objective is to design a lossless source code, with implied probability model $Q_{\Pi \g \SetDataSet}$, for the assignments $\Pi$ that can be used alongside any codec for the data set $\SetDataSet$.
elements.sent-2411-764: In what follows, we first describe the coding procedure defining the model $Q_{\Pi \g \SetDataSet}$.
elements.sent-2411-765: We then show the model resulting from our procedure assigns probability proportional to the product of cluster sizes.
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{assets/fig-method-rcc.pdf} \caption { High-level description of our method, Random Cycle Coding (RCC). RCC encodes the clustering $(\tilde{\X}, \Pi)$ as cycles in the permutation $\sigma$ induced by the relative ordering of objects. Left: Indices represent the rankings of objects in $\tilde{\X}$ according to the total ordering of $\X$. Middle: One of two permutations, in Foata's space, will be randomly selected with bits-back coding to represent the clustering. Right: The objects of $\tilde{\X}$ are encoded according to the selected permutation, implicitly encoding the clusters $\Pi$ in the permutation's disjoint cycles.} \label{fig:method-rcc}
elements.sent-2411-766: Our strategy will be to send the elements of $\SetDataSet$ in a particular ordering such that it implicitly encodes the clustering information.
elements.sent-2411-767: To achieve this, we view cluster assignments as partitions of the integer interval $[n]$.
elements.sent-2411-768: Each ordering of the data set will induce a cluster assignment via the cycles of an associated permutation.
elements.sent-2411-769: We associate a permutation $\sigma_{x^n}$ to each of the possible $n!$ orderings of $\SetDataSet$ based on sorting.
elements.sent-2411-770: Define $s^n$ to be a reference sequence created by sorting the elements in $\SetDataSet$ according to the total ordering $\leq$ of $\X$, \begin{equation}s_i \in \SetDataSet \quadsuch that\quad s_1 < s_2 < \dots < s_n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-771: Let $\SetOrders$ be the set of all possible orderings of $\SetDataSet$ created from the action of permutations in $\Sym_n$ on the reference sequence, \begin{equation}\label{eq:set-orders} \SetOrders = \left\{(s_{\sigma(1)}, \dots, s_{\sigma(n)}) \colon \sigma \in \Sym_n\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-772: For any $x^n \in \SetOrders$, the induced permutation $\sigma_{x^n}$ is defined as that which permutes the elements of the reference $s^n$ such that $x^n$ is obtained, \begin{equation}\left(s_{\sigma_{x^n}(1)}, \dots, s_{\sigma_{x^n}(n)}\right) = (x_1, \dots, x_n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-773: Under this definition the induced permutation can also be constructed by directly substituting $x_i$ for its ranking in $\SetDataSet$.
elements.sent-2411-774: The following equivalence relation helps us establish the relationship between permutations and cluster assignments.
elements.sent-2411-775: Two sequences $x^n, y^n \in \SetOrders$ are equivalent, written $x^n \sim y^n$, if the disjoint cycles of their induced permutations contain the same elements.
elements.sent-2411-777: The sequences are equivalent as the disjoint cycles of the induced permutations contain the same elements.
elements.sent-2411-778: For both sequences, elements $2, 4$, and $6$ are in the same cycle, while $8$ is in a cycle of its own.
elements.sent-2411-779: With this definition we can group orderings into equivalent sets, where all orderings in the same set induce the same partitioning of $[n]$.
elements.sent-2411-780: This is known as the quotient set, with elements called equivalence classes, \begin{equation}\SetOrders/{\sim} \defeq \left\{E \colon x^n, y^n \in E  if, and only if,  \sigma_{x^n} \sim \sigma_{y^n},  for all  x^n, y^n \in \SetOrders \right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-102: \begin{equation}\SetOrders/{\sim} \defeq \left\{E \colon x^n, y^n \in E  if, and only if,  \sigma_{x^n} \sim \sigma_{y^n},  for all  x^n, y^n \in \SetOrders \right\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-781: From \Cref{def:clustering we know each cluster assignment is equivalent to a partition on $[n]$.}
elements.sent-2411-782: We can therefore interpret each equivalence class as a cluster assignment and vice-versa.
elements.sent-2411-783: Then $\Pi$ can be redefined, without loss of generality, as a random variable taking on values in the quotient set $\quotient{\SetOrders {\sim}$, where all orderings of $\SetDataSet$ that induce the same partitioning/assignments belong to the same equivalence class.}
elements.sent-2411-784: The size of an equivalence class $\Pi \in \quotient{\SetOrders {\sim}$ can be computed by counting permutations with the same elements in each cycle.}
elements.sent-2411-785: Given a permutation, shifting one cycle left or right, with wrap-around, does not change the cycle.
elements.sent-2411-786: To generate a new permutation, we can fix one element of a cycle and permute the rest.
elements.sent-2411-787: For a cluster assignment with $\ell$ clusters and $n_i$ elements in each cluster, the logarithm of the size of the equivalence class is therefore, \begin{equation}\label{eq:logpi} \log\abs{\Pi} = \sum_{i=1}^\ell \log\left((n_i-1)!\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-788: Similar to Random Order Coding, Random Cycle Coding uses bits-back to select a representative of the equivalence class to be encoded in place of $\Pi$, which is composed of all elements of the data set $\SetDataSet$.
elements.sent-2411-789: To achieve the wanted optimality, RCC must remove exactly $\log\abs{\Pi}$ bits from the ANS state while encoding the representative sequence.
elements.sent-2411-790: At each step, an element from $\SetDataSet$ is selected, using an ANS decode operation, and then encoded using an arbitrary codec (which we will call the symbol codec).
elements.sent-2411-791: Interleaving decoding/sampling and encoding avoids the initial bits issue \cite{townsend2019practical resulting from the initially empty ANS state as discussed in \Cref{sec:bbans-initial-bits .}}
elements.sent-2411-792: Random Order Coding \cite{severo2023compressing (ROC) performs a similar procedure for multiset compression where elements are also sampled without replacement from $\SetDataSet$.}
elements.sent-2411-793: However, there the equivalence classes consist of all permutations over $\SetDataSet$, and therefore sampling can be done by picking any element from $\SetDataSet$ uniformly at random.
elements.sent-2411-794: RCC requires sampling without replacement from $\SetDataSet$ non-uniformly such that the resulting permutation has a desired cycle structure (i.e., such that the resulting sequence is in the correct equivalence class).
elements.sent-2411-795: This can be done with the following extension of Foata's Bijection \cite{foata1968netto .}
elements.sent-2411-796: First, write the permutation in disjoint cycle notation and sort the elements within each cycle, in ascending order, yielding a new permutation.
elements.sent-2411-797: Next, sort the cycles, based on the first (i.e., smallest) element, in descending order.
elements.sent-2411-798: Applying Foata's Canonicalization to either $\sigma$ or $\pi$ yields $(2\ 4\ 5)(1\ 3)$, which is equal to $\sigma$.
elements.algorithm-2411-005: Inputs: \begin{itemize} \item Assignment as a list of lists $\Pi = [[x^1_1, \dots, x^1_{n_1}], [x^2_1, \dots, x^2_{n_2}], \dots, [x^\ell_1, \dots, x^\ell_{n_\ell}]]$ sorted according to Foata's Theorem \item Initial ANS state \item Symbol codec \end{itemize} \For{$c = \ell, \dots, 1$}{ \nl Encode $\left\{x^c_2, \dots, x^c_{n_c}\right\}$ with ROC using the given symbol codec \nl Encode $x^c_1$ with symbol codec } \Return Final ANS state \caption{Pseudo-code for encoding with RCC.} \label{alg:rcc-encode}
elements.algorithm-2411-006: Inputs: \begin{itemize} \item Total number of elements $n$ \item Final ANS state, constructed from \Cref{alg:rcc-encode} \item Symbol Codec \end{itemize} Initialize $\Pi = [\ ], c=0$ \While{total number of elements in $\Pi$ is less than $n$}{ Decode $x^c_1$ with the symbol codec Decode elements $x^c_i$ with ROC until an element smaller than $x^c_1$ is seen Add all decoded elements to $\Pi$ as a list $[x^c_1, \dots, x^c_{n_c}]$ Increment $c$ } \Return $\Pi$, Initial ANS state \caption{Pseudo-code for decoding with RCC.} \label{alg:rcc-decode}

elements.sent-2411-818: The implied probability model only depends on the cluster sizes, and assigns higher probability when there are few clusters with many elements, \begin{equation}\label{eq:rcc-probability-model} Q_{\Pi \g \SetDataSet}(\Pi \g \SetDataSet) = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^k (n_i -1)!}{n!},\end{equation} where $n_i$ is the size of the $i$-th cluster, and $k$ the total number of clusters.

elements.sent-2411-824: To the best of our knowledge there is no other method which can perform lossless compression of clustered high-dimensional data.

elements.sent-2411-867: This chapter provides an efficient lossless coding algorithm for storing random clusters of objects from arbitrary sets.
elements.sent-2411-868: Our method, Random Cycle Coding (RCC), stores the clustering information in the ordering between encoded objects and does away with the need to assign meaningless labels for storage purposes.
elements.sent-2411-869: RCC implicitly encodes clustering information in the disjoint cycles of the permutation defined by the relative ordering between data points.
elements.sent-2411-870: RCC randomly picks the order in which data points are encoded into the ANS state, while simultaneously guaranteeing that each disjoint cycle, of the permutation defined by the ordering, has the same elements as some cluster.
elements.sent-2411-871: We show that a random clustering can be decomposed into 2 distinct mathematical quantities, the data set of objects present in the clusters (i.e., the union of all clusters), and an equivalence class representing the cluster assignments.
elements.sent-2411-872: For a given clustering, the equivalence class contains all possible orderings of the data that have cycles with the same elements as some cluster.
elements.sent-2411-873: The logarithm of the equivalence class size is exactly the number of bits needed to communicate an ordering of the data points, with the wanted permutation cycles, which we refer to as the order information.
elements.sent-2411-874: This quantity was previously defined by \cite{varshney2006toward as the amount of bits required to communicate an ordering of a sequence if the multiset of symbols was given, and equaled $\log n!$ when there are no repeated symbols.}
elements.sent-2411-875: In the cluster case, the order information $\log\abs{\Pi}$ is strictly less than $\log n!$ as the clustering carries partial information regarding the ordering between symbols in the following way: given the clustering, only orderings with the corresponding cycle structure will be communicated.
elements.sent-2411-876: The savings achieved by RCC equals exactly the assignment information of the data, implying RCC is optimal in terms of compression rate for the probability model shown in \Cref{eq:rcc-probability-model .}
elements.sent-2411-877: The computational complexity of RCC scales with the number of bits recovered by bits-back, and reverts back to that of compressing a sequence when all clusters are atomic.
elements.sent-2411-878: The savings for RCC scales quasi-linearly with the cluster sizes, and is independent of the representation size of the data.
elements.sent-2411-879: The experiments on real-world datasets from vector similarity search databases showcases where we think our method is most attractive: clusters of data requiring few bytes per element to communicate, where the bits-back savings can represent a significant share of the total representation size.
elements.sent-2411-880: Network data, such as social networks and web graphs \cite{newman2018networks , can be represented as large graphs, or multigraphs, with millions of nodes and edges corresponding to members of a population and their interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-881: To preserve the underlying community structure, as well as other relational properties, algorithms for compressing and storing network graphs must be lossless.
elements.sent-2411-882: The entropy coding of a network can, in general, be done in quadratic time with respect to the number of vertices by storing binary variables indicating the absence or presence of each possible edge in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-883: Most real-world networks are sparse in the sense that the number of edges $m$ is significantly smaller than the maximum number of possible edges $\binom{n {2}$ in a simple graph \cite{newman2018networks .}}
elements.sent-2411-884: A graph that is not sparse is known as a dense graph.
elements.sent-2411-885: Algorithms with sub-quadratic complexity in the number of observed edges are more attractive for compressing network data than those that scale quadratically with the number of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-886: Some networks exhibit small-world characteristics where most nodes are not connected by an edge but the degree of separation of any 2 nodes is small \cite{newman2018networks .}

elements.sent-2411-889: This chapter presents a lossless source code for large labeled graphs called Random Edge Coding (REC).
elements.sent-2411-890: REC is optimal under a broad class of distributions referred to as edge-permutation invariant (\Cref{def:rec-edge-permutation-invariance ) and can achieve competitive performance on real-world networks as we show in \Cref{sec:rec-experiments .}}

elements.sent-2411-892: REC is applicable to both simple and non-simple labeled graphs, with directed or undirected edges, as well as hyper-graphs.
elements.sent-2411-893: REC uses bits-back coding \cite{frey1996free,townsend2019practical to sample edges and vertices without replacement from the graph's edge-list, similar to Random Order Coding (ROC) and Random Cycle Coding (RCC).}

elements.algorithm-2411-008: \caption{Random Edge Encoder} \label{alg:bb-exg} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} Vertex sequence $v^{2m}$ and ANS state $s$. \STATE 1) Edge-sort the vertex sequence $v^{2m}$ \REPEAT \STATE 2) Decode an edge $e_k$ uniformly from the sequence \STATE 3) Remove $e_k$ from the vertex sequence \STATE 4) Decode a binary vertex-index $b$ uniformly in $\{0, 1\}$ \STATE 5) Encode $e_k$ \STATE 6) Encode $e_k[1-b]$ \UNTIL{The vertex sequence is empty} \STATE 7) Encode $m$ using $\log m$ bits. \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-1006: In this section, we showcase the optimality of REC on large graphs representing real-world networks.
elements.sent-2411-1007: We entropy code with REC using Pólya's Urn (PU) model and compare the performance to state-of-the-art compression algorithms tailored to network compression.
elements.sent-2411-1008: We report the average number of bits required to represent an edge in the graph (i.e., bits-per-edge) as is common in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-1009: We use the ANS implementation available in Craystack \cite{craystack .}
elements.sent-2411-1010: Code implementing Random Edge Coding, Pólya's Urn model, and experiments are available at \url{https://github.com/dsevero/Random-Edge-Coding .}
elements.sent-2411-1011: We used datasets containing simple network graphs with small-world statistics such as YouTube, FourSquare, Gowalla, and Digg \cite{rossi2015network which are expected to have high probability under PU.}
elements.sent-2411-1012: As negative examples, we compress Skitter and DBLP networks \cite{snapnets , where we expect the results to be significantly worse than the state of the art, as these networks lack small-world statistics.}
elements.sent-2411-1013: The smallest network (Gowalla) has roughly $200$ thousand nodes and $1$ million edges, while the largest (YouTube) has more than $3$ million nodes and almost $10$ million edges.
elements.sent-2411-1014: The cost of sending the number of edges $m$ is negligible but is accounted for in the calculation of the bits-per-edge by adding $32$ bits.
elements.sent-2411-1015: To compress a graph, the edges are loaded into memory as a list where each element is an edge represented by a tuple containing two vertex elements (integers).
elements.sent-2411-1016: At each step, an edge is sampled without replacement using an ANS decode operation as described in \Cref{alg:bb-exg .}
elements.sent-2411-1017: Encoding is performed in a depth-first fashion, where an edge is encoded to completion before moving on to another.
elements.sent-2411-1018: Then, a vertex is sampled without replacement from the edge and entropy-encoded using \eqref{eq:fhm-conditional .}
elements.sent-2411-1019: The process repeats until the edge is depleted, and then starts again by sampling another edge without replacement.
elements.sent-2411-1020: The process terminates once the edge-list is empty, concluding the encoding of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-1021: Decoding is performed in reverse order and yields a vertex sequence that is equivalent (i.e., maps to the same graph as) the original graph.
elements.sent-2411-1022: We showcase the optimality of REC by compressing real-world graphs to the negative log-probability under the Pólya's Urn (PU) model.
elements.sent-2411-1023: As discussed in \Cref{subsec:rates-below-entropy-are-achievable the graph's NLOGP is the value an optimal entropy coder should achieve to minimize the average number of bits with respect to the model for large sequence lengths.}
elements.sent-2411-1024: REC can compress the graph to its NLOGP (as indicated by the last column of \Cref{table:rec-optimality ) for all datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-1025: Compressing the graph as a sequence of vertices (i.e., without REC) would require a number of bits-per-edge equal to the sequence's NLOGP, which is significantly higher than the NLOGP of the graph as can be seen by the first column of \Cref{table:rec-optimality .}
elements.sent-2411-1026: As these methods evolve to achieve better probability values the compression performance is expected to improve automatically due to the optimality of REC.
elements.sent-2411-1027: In \Cref{table:rec-results we compare the bits-per-edge achieved by PU using REC with current state-of-the-art algorithms for network data.}
elements.sent-2411-1028: PU performs competitively on all social networks and can even outperform previous works on networks such as YouTube \cite{rossi2015network .}
elements.sent-2411-1029: The probability assigned by PU for non-social networks is expected to be low, resulting in poor compression performance, as indicated by the last 2 columns of \Cref{table:rec-results .}
elements.sent-2411-1030: The performance of PU deteriorates as the edge density increases and is visible from \Cref{table:rec-results .}
elements.sent-2411-1031: While the compression with REC is optimal for PU, the final results depend on the probability assigned to the graph under PU, which is why ad hoc methods can achieve better performance.
elements.sent-2411-1032: Nonetheless, the bits-per-edge of PU with REC is close to that of current methods.
elements.sent-2411-1033: In this chapter we developed an algorithm capable of performing lossless coding with ANS of large edge-permutation invariant graphs: Random Edge Coding (REC).
elements.sent-2411-1034: We provide an example use case with the self-reinforcing Pólya's Urn model \cite{mahmoud2008polya which performs competitively with state of the art methods despite having $0$ parameters and being void of floating-point arithmetic.}
elements.sent-2411-1035: This optimality implies that the efforts from the graph modeling community in improving the probability of network data under edge-permutation invariant models can be directly translated into an increased performance in lossless compression tasks, as Random Edge Coding can compress graphs to the theoretical optimum (negative log-probability) for any EPI data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-1036: Any model satisfying EPI can be used for optimal compression with REC, including neural network based models.
elements.sent-2411-1037: Learning exchangeable models has been explored in the literature \cite{niepert2014exchangeable, bloem2020probabilistic but, to the best of our knowledge, using them for compression of graphs and other structured data is an under-explored field.}
elements.sent-2411-1038: Pólya's Urn satisfies edge-permutation invariance through the invariance of the PMF to permutations of the vertices, which is a sufficient, but not necessary, condition.

elements.sent-2411-1040: One way to achieve this is through the general framework of \cite{zaheer2017deep, hartford2018deep, meng2019hats .}

elements.chapter-2411-009: Coding Combinatorial Random Variables with Random Permutation Codes
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{assets/fig-crv-examples.pdf} \caption{Varying the definition of the equivalence relation $\sim$ yields a different combinatorial random variable $X = [Z^n]$ in each column.} \label{fig:crv-examples}
elements.sent-2411-1053: This chapter concludes the thesis by unifying previous algorithms under a common framework.
elements.sent-2411-1054: The previous chapters described algorithms to compress non-sequential data by encoding data points, in one-of-many possible orderings, with codes from the bits-back family \cite{townsend2019practical,frey1996free .}
elements.sent-2411-1055: The common element between all algorithms is the view of the non-sequential object as an equivalence class, with the equivalence relation changing depending on the type of object being compressed.
elements.sent-2411-1056: In the case of ROC, the equivalence relation groups together sequences that are equal up to a permutation of their elements; the equivalence classes are therefore interpreted as multisets (\Cref{def:multisets ).}
elements.sent-2411-1057: Similarly, clusters/partitions for RCC, and all graph types discussed for REC, are defined via equivalence relations which are finer (\Cref{def:finer-equivalence-relation ) than the equivalence relation defining multisets, i.e., permutation-equivalence.}
elements.sent-2411-1058: This chapter presents a unifying view through Combinatorial Random Variables (CRV, \Cref{def:combinatorial-random-variables ); a random variable with alphabet equal to the quotient set under some equivalence relation finer than permutation-equivalence.}
elements.sent-2411-1059: Restricting the class of equivalence classes allows for the construction of efficient lossless source codes for CRVs, and allows us to characterize the achievable rates in the large state ANS regime.
elements.sent-2411-1060: Random Permutation Codes (RPCs) are presented as a family of algorithms, which include ROC, RCC, and REC, capable of achieving the optimal rates, in the large ANS state regime, for general CRVs.
elements.sent-2411-1061: Finally, the rate achieved by RPCs is shown to be equal to the Negative Evidence Lower Bound \cite{jordan1999introduction , a well studied quantity in the literature of variational inference.}
elements.sent-2411-1062: This establishes the view that RPCs implicitly make use of a latent variable model, achieving the rates given in \Cref{lemma:rate-for-lvm-with-ans .}
elements.sent-2411-1063: Then, the following binary relation $\sim$ is an equivalence relation (\Cref{def:equivalence-relation ).}
elements.sent-2411-1064: Let $z^n \sim w^n$ if, and only if, there exists a multiset-permutation $\sigma$ on $n$ elements such that, \begin{equation}\left( z_{\sigma(1)}, \dots, z_{\sigma(n)} \right) = (w_1, \dots, w_n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1065: Sequences obeying this relationship are said to be permutation-equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-1066: A CRV is any random variable with alphabet equal to the quotient set $\quotient{\Z^n {\sim}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1067: This definition states the instances of CRVs are equivalence classes under the given equivalence relation $\sim$.
elements.sent-2411-1068: The alphabet $\Z$ can be any countable set such as the set of images, all positive integers, or even a quotient set of some other CRV.
elements.sent-2411-1069: Every equivalence class under $\sim$ is a subset of an equivalence class under permutation-equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-1070: A wide range of data types can be recovered by varying the definition of $\sim$, $n$, and $\Z$, including multisets, partitions, graphs, hypergraphs, trees and many others.
elements.sent-2411-1071: We give examples next assuming $\Z$ is an arbitrary set (unless stated otherwise) and $z^n, w^n \in \Z^n$.

elements.sent-2411-1076: Specifying a distribution over sequences in $\Z^n$ defines a distribution for the CRV.
elements.sent-2411-1077: It is sufficient to consider distributions over sequences that assign equal mass to equivalent sequences, as the following lemma characterizes.
elements.sent-2411-1078: For any $P_X$ there exists a distribution $Z^n \sim P_{Z^n}$ that assigns equal mass to equivalent sequences, \begin{equation}\label{eq:crv-exchangeable} P_{Z^n}(z^n) = P_{Z^n}(w^n)  if  z^n \sim w^n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1079: Given $P_X$, construct the following distribution assigning equal mass to all sequences in the equivalence class, \begin{equation}P_{Z^n}(z^n) = \abs{[z^n]}^{-1} P_X([z^n]).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1080: A distribution satisfying \Cref{eq:crv-exchangeable is said to be exchangeable under $\sim$.}
elements.sent-2411-1081: Despite this sufficiency result, it can be useful in practice to use non-exchangeable distributions if they offer a computational advantage in computing and range values required for coding with ANS.

elements.sent-2411-1090: This section shows a unifying view of these algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-1091: We refer to this family of algorithms as Random Permutation Codes.
elements.sent-2411-1092: In practice, a sequence in the equivalence class is compressed to represent the combinatorial object in digital mediums.

elements.sent-2411-1147: The equivalence relation in the definition of a CRV is restricted to be finer, or as fine as, permutation-equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-1148: This allows us to make strong statements regarding the achievable rates of CRVs, as well as develop the family of RPCs that can efficiently achieve these rates.
elements.sent-2411-1149: A suggestion for future work is to relax this requirement to allow arbitrary equivalence relations.
elements.sent-2411-1150: This would permit modeling other, more complex, objects such as unlabelled graph, where graphs are equivalent up to a permutation of their vertex labels that preserves the edges in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-1151: Similarly, another direction is to change the restriction to be an equivalence relation coarser, or as coarse as, permutation-equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-1152: This would mean that the equivalence classes of the CRV can be written as the disjoint union of equivalence classes under permutation-equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-1153: The advantage of this direction is the potential to re-use RPCs as sub-routines in the to be developed compression algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-1154: The coarseness, discussed previously, need not be with respect to permutation-equivalence, but instead any of the previously discussed equivalence relations such as those underlying REC or RCC.
elements.sent-2411-1155: However, it is unclear what type of real-world non-sequential objects could be modeled with this construction.
elements.sent-2411-1156: Finally, as a last remark on potential future work, this manuscript deals exclusively with lossless source coding, and does not investigate the limits of lossy source coding: such as when some distortion can be tolerated in the sequences $Z^n$ or equivalence classes $X$.. \backmatter.
